nativeRange.habitat: Originally described from Panama; widely distributed in warm coastal lagoons, estuaries, mangrove systems, and inland saline waters across the Americas, the Caribbean, and parts of the Indo-Pacific. Generally found in shallow brackish-to-marine waters with abundant phytoplankton, microbial biofilms, and detritus.

decorPreferences: bare-bottom culture container or in-tank refugium
gentle aeration (no fine bubbles that strip pods at the surface)
no mechanical filtration on the culture vessel (sponge pre-filter only)
ambient or low lighting on the culture
no copper, zinc, or brass plumbing or decor in contact with the water

diet.notes: Apocyclops is an omnivore that has been reported to consume phytoplankton species such as Nannochloropsis, Isochrysis, Tetraselmis, and Chlorella, along with diatoms, cyanobacteria, microbial biofilms, and fine detritus. One practical advantage over Parvocalanus is that Apocyclops has been reported to thrive on prepared algae pastes — the same concentrated pastes used to culture rotifers — which eliminates the need to maintain a continuous live-phytoplankton system alongside the copepod culture. Phytoplankton or algae paste is generally advised to be dosed one to three times per week; over-feeding should be avoided because excess organic matter can quickly foul the culture water.

goodWith: monoculture only (kept as a feed-culture / refugium population, not a display animal)
harvested to feed water-column-feeding fish such as wild seahorses, mandarin dragonets, anthias, fairy wrasses, chromis, and pipefish
used as a first food for marine ornamental fish larvae (e.g. flame angelfish, clownfish, dottybacks)
filter-feeding corals and other zooplanktivorous reef invertebrates
compatible with macroalgae refugia (Chaetomorpha, Caulerpa) as long-term in-system habitat
avoidWith: any predator in the culture container (will rapidly crash the culture)
copper-based medications, brass/copper fittings, or galvanised metal in the culture water
strong mechanical filtration or fine bubbles that strip pods from the water column
untreated tap water (chlorine and chloramine are acutely toxic)

summary: The Apocyclops Pod (Apocyclops panamensis) is a tiny cyclopoid copepod commercially cultured as a live food for water-column-feeding marine fish — including mandarins, seahorses, and anthias — and as a first food for newly hatched ornamental fish larvae. More pelagic than Tigriopus or Tisbe, its free-swimming nauplii are accessible to fish that cannot yet chase bottom-dwelling pods.
careNotes: The Apocyclops Pod is kept primarily as a feed culture or a refugium-seeding population rather than a display animal. Adults are tear-drop-shaped cyclopoids generally reported at roughly 0.8-1.3 mm total length, with females noticeably larger than males. The first larval stage — nauplii — measures around 50-100 micrometres, small enough to be accepted by very small marine fish larvae that cannot yet take Parvocalanus or Tisbe nauplii. Unlike Tigriopus (a strictly benthic harpacticoid that grazes surfaces) and Tisbe (a benthic interstitial species), Apocyclops is semi-pelagic — nauplii and juveniles are planktonic in the water column and adults migrate between substrate and column, which is why they are specifically preferred for water-column-feeding fish such as seahorses, mandarin dragonets, anthias, fairy wrasses, and pipefish, and for pelagic-spawning ornamental fish larvae (flame angels, clownfish, dottybacks). The species is highly euryhaline (has been reported from near-freshwater to around 60 ppt) and eurythermal (15-35°C survival range), but the cultured commercial strain is typically marketed for brackish-to-full-marine reef conditions; culture is generally advised at 22-28°C, pH 7.8-8.4, and reef-strength salinity (SG 1.020-1.026) with stable parameters. Has been reported to reproduce roughly twice as fast as Tigriopus and Tisbe under good conditions, which supports rapid refugium seeding. Cultures are generally kept in small bare-bottom containers ranging from a 4-8 L jar up to around a 20 L tank, or seeded directly into a display-tank refugium with macroalgae and live rock rubble. Like all crustaceans, Apocyclops has been reported to be acutely sensitive to copper-based medications and to chlorine or chloramine; phytoplankton should be dosed only into dechlorinated water, and the culture should never be exposed to copper plumbing or treatments.
breedingNotes: Reproduces continuously in culture, generally reported to roughly double the reproduction rate of Tigriopus and Tisbe under similar conditions. Population doubling has been reported in the order of every 1-3 weeks under good food and stable temperature. Development passes through six naupliar and five copepodite stages before reaching the adult, with each stage accompanied by a molt; nauplii are planktonic in the water column and become more benthic as they mature. Generally advised to harvest a third or less of the culture at any time to maintain reproductive momentum, and to keep two or more backup cultures because a single crash can wipe out the supply.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Designated primary did not surface a dedicated Apocyclops panamensis product page during this research run; the homepage and search returned only general LiveAquaria content. Substituted AlgaeBarn (a recognised primary copepod supplier with a detailed species write-up) as the functional primary.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn - A Closer Look at the Cyclopoid Copepod Apocyclops panamensis
additional.notes: Functional primary substitute (designated primary LiveAquaria did not surface a dedicated species page; designated secondary Reef2Reef Wiki returned HTTP 403). Source for taxonomy (cyclopoid copepod), 'between Tisbe and Tigriopus in size,' nauplii under 100 micrometres, eurythermal, planktonic young / benthic adults, reproduction roughly twice that of Tisbe and Tigriopus, omnivore preferring diatoms and cyanobacteria, astaxanthin colour-enhancement, target fish including mandarins and seahorses.

additional.name: Wikipedia - Apocyclops panamensis
additional.notes: Taxonomy reference (family Cyclopidae, order Cyclopoida, synonym Cyclops panamensis Marsh 1913), native-range / habitat description (coastal lagoons, estuaries, mangrove systems, inland saline waters across the Americas, Caribbean, Indo-Pacific), adult female 0.8-1.1 mm total length, six naupliar + five copepodite stages, brackish/marine coastal species.

additional.name: PodDrop - Apocyclops Panamensis Live Copepods For Sale
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 0.8-1.3 mm with females larger than males, temperature optimum 25-30°C (survival 15-35°C), pH 7.5-8.5 optimal, salinity tolerance 0-60 ppt with optimal reproduction at 15-35 ppt, semi-pelagic water-column behaviour distinguishing this species from strictly benthic Tigriopus / Tisbe.

additional.name: PodYourReef - Apocyclops panamensis: A Scientific and Practical Guide for Reef Aquarists
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed 24-28°C tropical husbandry range, omnivory with preference for diatoms and cyanobacteria, target species (mandarins, dragonets, wrasses, seahorses, chromis, anthias), refugium compatibility with macroalgae and rock rubble. Reported adult size (0.4-0.6 mm) was lower than other sources and was averaged out against Wikipedia + PodDrop in favour of the 0.8-1.3 mm consensus.

additional.name: CORAL Magazine - Apocyclops Poised to Revolutionize Marine Fish Aquaculture
additional.notes: Aquaculture context: A. panamensis has been used as a first food for first-feeding Flame Angelfish (Centropyge loricula) and other pelagic-spawning marine ornamentals; key practical advantage is culturability on prepared algae pastes, eliminating live-phytoplankton dependency that constrains Parvocalanus crassirostris.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Copepods molt at every developmental transition; Apocyclops nauplii and copepodites pass through six naupliar and five copepodite molts before reaching the adult stage, with each molt typically separated by a day or two at culture temperature. Shed exoskeletons contribute fine particulates to the culture water and are widely considered normal.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Reproduces rapidly under good conditions, has been reported to roughly double the reproduction rate of Tigriopus and Tisbe. See top-level breedingNotes for the full culture protocol.
